toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Taxonomy Patterns

ASG detects and maps four distinct taxonomy patterns from OWL ontologies to OpenAPI schemas. Each pattern represents a different way to model enumerated or categorized values.

## Pattern Overview

| Pattern | OWL Structure | OpenAPI Output | Use Case |
|---------|---------------|----------------|----------|
| **A** | `owl:oneOf` with untyped individuals | Inline string enum | Simple, closed enumeration |
| **B** | `owl:oneOf` with typed individuals | Inline string enum | Closed enumeration with metadata |
| **C** | Named class (no SKOS) | `$ref` to schema | Extensible, hierarchical taxonomy |
| **D** | SKOS-based class | Inline string enum | Standards-compliant vocabulary |

## Pattern Detection

ASG automatically detects the appropriate pattern based on the OWL structure:

```
OWL Restriction
├── owl:oneOf (blank node)?
│   ├── Individuals have domain class? → Pattern B
│   └── Untyped individuals only → Pattern A
└── Named class IRI?
    ├── Subclass of skos:Concept or has skos:inScheme? → Pattern D
    └── No object property restrictions? → Pattern C
```

---

## Pattern A: Inline Enumeration (Untyped Individuals)

Use Pattern A for simple, closed enumerations where you just need a list of valid values.

### When to Use

- Fixed set of values that rarely changes
- No metadata needed per value
- Values are simple labels

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- Untyped individuals (only owl:NamedIndividual) -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/Active">
    <rdfs:label>Active</rdfs:label>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/Inactive">
    <rdfs:label>Inactive</rdfs:label>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/Pending">
    <rdfs:label>Pending</rdfs:label>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction with owl:oneOf -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasStatus"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom>
            <owl:Class>
                <owl:oneOf rdf:parseType="Collection">
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/Active"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/Inactive"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/Pending"/>
                </owl:oneOf>
            </owl:Class>
        </owl:someValuesFrom>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
hasStatus:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    type: string
    enum:
      - Active
      - Inactive
      - Pending
```

### Cardinality Variations

| OWL Restriction | OpenAPI Effect |
|----------------|----------------|
| `someValuesFrom` | `type: array`, `minItems: 1`, required |
| `allValuesFrom` | `type: array` (optional) |
| `qualifiedCardinality(1)` | Non-array, required |
| `maxQualifiedCardinality(1)` | Non-array (optional) |
| `minQualifiedCardinality(n)` | `type: array`, `minItems: n`, required |

---

## Pattern B: Typed Individuals with Metadata

Use Pattern B when you need individuals to have a domain class and potentially carry metadata like code values, definitions, or display names.

### When to Use

- Values need to be typed (instances of a specific class)
- Values may have metadata (codeValue, displayName, definition)
- You want to query individuals via SPARQL using their type

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- Domain class for the enum values -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeCode">
    <skos:prefLabel>Sensor Type Code</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Typed individuals with metadata -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeAnalog">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/SensorTypeCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Analog</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Analog sensor type.</skos:definition>
    <example:codeValue>analog</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeDigital">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/SensorTypeCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Digital</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Digital sensor type.</skos:definition>
    <example:codeValue>digital</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction with owl:oneOf -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasSensorType"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom>
            <owl:Class>
                <owl:oneOf rdf:parseType="Collection">
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeAnalog"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeDigital"/>
                </owl:oneOf>
            </owl:Class>
        </owl:someValuesFrom>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
hasSensorType:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    type: string
    enum:
      - Analog
      - Digital
```

### Pattern A vs B Comparison

| Aspect | Pattern A | Pattern B |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Individual typing | `owl:NamedIndividual` only | Domain class + metadata |
| SPARQL query | Parse `owl:oneOf` | `?x a SensorTypeCode` |
| Metadata support | `rdfs:label` only | Full annotation support |
| API filtering | Not supported | Via `apiLabel` on individuals |

---

## Pattern C: Class-Based Taxonomy (Extensible)

Use Pattern C for extensible taxonomies where values can be added without modifying the restriction, and optionally support hierarchical structure.

### When to Use

- Values should be discoverable via class membership
- New values can be added without changing the schema
- Hierarchical grouping is needed (via subclasses)
- You want a reusable schema in components/schemas

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- Parent taxonomy class -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode">
    <skos:prefLabel>Environment Code</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Operating environment classification.</skos:definition>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Subclasses for hierarchy (optional) -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/IndoorEnvironment">
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Indoor Environment</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/OutdoorEnvironment">
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Outdoor Environment</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Individuals (instances of subclasses) -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/EnvOffice">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/IndoorEnvironment"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Office</skos:prefLabel>
    <example:codeValue>office</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/EnvOutdoor">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/OutdoorEnvironment"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Outdoor</skos:prefLabel>
    <example:codeValue>outdoor</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction to named class (NOT owl:oneOf) -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasEnvironment"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
# On the resource
hasEnvironment:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    $ref: '#/components/schemas/EnvironmentCode'

# In components/schemas
EnvironmentCode:
  type: object
  properties:
    code:
      type: string
      enum:
        - office
        - outdoor
    displayName:
      type: string
```

### Flat vs Hierarchical

Pattern C supports both flat taxonomies (direct instances of the class) and hierarchical taxonomies (instances of subclasses).

**Flat Taxonomy:**
```
EnvironmentCode
├── EnvOffice (instance)
├── EnvWarehouse (instance)
└── EnvOutdoor (instance)
```

**Hierarchical Taxonomy:**
```
EnvironmentCode
├── IndoorEnvironment (subclass)
│   ├── EnvOffice (instance)
│   └── EnvWarehouse (instance)
└── OutdoorEnvironment (subclass)
    └── EnvOutdoor (instance)
```

### Pattern B vs C Comparison

| Aspect | Pattern B | Pattern C |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Restriction target | Blank node with `owl:oneOf` | Named class IRI |
| Set closure | Closed (explicit list) | Open (query instances) |
| Adding values | Requires changing `owl:oneOf` | Just add instance |
| Hierarchy | Flat only | Supports subclass hierarchy |
| OpenAPI output | Inline enum | `$ref` to schema |

---

## Pattern D: SKOS Taxonomy

Use Pattern D for standards-compliant vocabularies using SKOS (Simple Knowledge Organization System), which enables interoperability with terminology servers and FHIR-compatible systems.

### When to Use

- Standards compliance required (SKOS, FHIR)
- Multi-language support needed (`skos:prefLabel` with `@lang`)
- Hierarchical relationships (`skos:broader`, `skos:narrower`)
- External code mapping (`skos:exactMatch`)
- Terminology server integration

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- SKOS ConceptScheme -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#ConceptScheme"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Measurement Context Code System</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Bridge class linking to SKOS scheme -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode">
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
    <owl:equivalentClass>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#inScheme"/>
            <owl:hasValue rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </owl:equivalentClass>
    <skos:prefLabel>Measurement Context Code</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>

<!-- SKOS Concepts -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/ContextContinuous">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode"/>
    <skos:inScheme rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
    <skos:notation>continuous</skos:notation>
    <skos:prefLabel>Continuous</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Continuous measurement context.</skos:definition>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/ContextDiscrete">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode"/>
    <skos:inScheme rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
    <skos:notation>discrete</skos:notation>
    <skos:prefLabel>Discrete</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Discrete measurement context.</skos:definition>
    <skos:narrower rdf:resource="https://example.com/ContextTriggered"/>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction to SKOS-based class -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasMeasurementContext"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
MeasurementContextCode:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    type: string
    enum:
      - Continuous
      - Discrete
      - Triggered
```

### SKOS Detection Criteria

ASG detects Pattern D when the target class:
- Is `rdfs:subClassOf skos:Concept`, OR
- Has an `owl:equivalentClass` with a `skos:inScheme` restriction

### Pattern C vs D Comparison

| Aspect | Pattern C (Class-based) | Pattern D (SKOS) |
|--------|-------------------------|------------------|
| Standard | OWL only | OWL + SKOS |
| Hierarchy | `rdfs:subClassOf` | `skos:broader/narrower` |
| Membership | `rdf:type` | `skos:inScheme` |
| Multi-language | Not native | `skos:prefLabel@en` |
| External mapping | Not native | `skos:exactMatch` |
| Discovery | `?x a/rdfs:subClassOf* Class` | `?x skos:inScheme Scheme` |

---

## Cardinality Summary

All patterns support the same cardinality restrictions:

| OWL Restriction | Required? | Array? | minItems | maxItems |
|-----------------|-----------|--------|----------|----------|
| `qualifiedCardinality(1)` | Yes | No | - | - |
| `maxQualifiedCardinality(1)` | No | No | - | - |
| `someValuesFrom` | Yes | Yes | 1 | - |
| `allValuesFrom` | No | Yes | - | - |
| `minQualifiedCardinality(n)` | Yes (if n≥1) | Yes | n | - |
| `maxQualifiedCardinality(n)` | No | Yes | - | n |

---

## Choosing the Right Pattern

| Requirement | Recommended Pattern |
|-------------|---------------------|
| Simple closed list, rarely changes | Pattern A |
| Typed values with metadata | Pattern B |
| Extensible, add values without schema change | Pattern C |
| Hierarchical grouping needed | Pattern C or D |
| Standards compliance (SKOS, FHIR) | Pattern D |
| Multi-language support | Pattern D |
| External terminology mapping | Pattern D |

input.new_string:
### OpenAPI Output

With `maxIndividualDepth >= 1` (default), Pattern B generates `oneOf` schemas with const objects:

```yaml
hasSensorType:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    oneOf:
      - type: object
        properties:
          code:
            const: "analog"
            type: string
          displayName:
            const: "Analog"
            type: string
          definition:
            const: "Analog sensor type."
            type: string
      - type: object
        properties:
          code:
            const: "digital"
            type: string
          displayName:
            const: "Digital"
            type: string
          definition:
            const: "Digital sensor type."
            type: string
```

### Object Property Assertions

When Pattern B individuals have object property assertions, these are expanded as nested objects (up to `maxIndividualDepth`):

**OWL:**
```xml
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeAnalog">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/SensorTypeCode"/>
    <skos:notation>analog</skos:notation>
    <example:hasCategory rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementCategory"/>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
```

**OpenAPI (depth 2):**
```yaml
oneOf:
  - type: object
    properties:
      code:
        const: "analog"
        type: string
      hasCategory:
        type: object
        properties:
          code:
            const: "measurement"
            type: string
```

### Cycle Detection

If object property assertions form a cycle (A → B → A), ASG detects this and uses a string reference instead of infinite nesting.

### Legacy Mode

Set `maxIndividualDepth: 0` to generate simple string enums (pre-enhancement behavior):

```yaml
hasSensorType:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    type: string
    enum:
      - analog
      - digital
```

### Pattern A vs B Comparison

| Aspect | Pattern A | Pattern B |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Individual typing | `owl:NamedIndividual` only | Domain class + metadata |
| OpenAPI output | Simple string enum | `oneOf` with const objects |
| Property assertions | Not supported | Expanded as nested objects |
| SPARQL query | Parse `owl:oneOf` | `?x a SensorTypeCode` |
| Metadata support | `rdfs:label` only | Full annotation + property support |

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Taxonomy Patterns

ASG detects and maps four distinct taxonomy patterns from OWL ontologies to OpenAPI schemas. Each pattern represents a different way to model enumerated or categorized values.

## Pattern Overview

| Pattern | OWL Structure | OpenAPI Output | Use Case |
|---------|---------------|----------------|----------|
| **A** | `owl:oneOf` with untyped individuals | Inline string enum | Simple, closed enumeration |
| **B** | `owl:oneOf` with typed individuals | `oneOf` with const objects | Closed enumeration with property graphs |
| **C** | Named class (no SKOS) | `$ref` to schema | Extensible, hierarchical taxonomy |
| **D** | SKOS-based class | Inline string enum | Standards-compliant vocabulary |

## Pattern Detection

ASG automatically detects the appropriate pattern based on the OWL structure:

```
OWL Restriction
├── owl:oneOf (blank node)?
│   ├── Individuals have domain class? → Pattern B
│   └── Untyped individuals only → Pattern A
└── Named class IRI?
    ├── Subclass of skos:Concept or has skos:inScheme? → Pattern D
    └── No object property restrictions? → Pattern C
```

---

## Pattern A: Inline Enumeration (Untyped Individuals)

Use Pattern A for simple, closed enumerations where you just need a list of valid values.

### When to Use

- Fixed set of values that rarely changes
- No metadata needed per value
- Values are simple labels

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- Untyped individuals (only owl:NamedIndividual) -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/Active">
    <rdfs:label>Active</rdfs:label>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/Inactive">
    <rdfs:label>Inactive</rdfs:label>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/Pending">
    <rdfs:label>Pending</rdfs:label>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction with owl:oneOf -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasStatus"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom>
            <owl:Class>
                <owl:oneOf rdf:parseType="Collection">
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/Active"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/Inactive"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/Pending"/>
                </owl:oneOf>
            </owl:Class>
        </owl:someValuesFrom>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
hasStatus:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    type: string
    enum:
      - Active
      - Inactive
      - Pending
```

### Cardinality Variations

| OWL Restriction | OpenAPI Effect |
|----------------|----------------|
| `someValuesFrom` | `type: array`, `minItems: 1`, required |
| `allValuesFrom` | `type: array` (optional) |
| `qualifiedCardinality(1)` | Non-array, required |
| `maxQualifiedCardinality(1)` | Non-array (optional) |
| `minQualifiedCardinality(n)` | `type: array`, `minItems: n`, required |

---

## Pattern B: Typed Individuals with Metadata

Use Pattern B when you need individuals to have a domain class and potentially carry metadata like code values, definitions, or display names.

### When to Use

- Values need to be typed (instances of a specific class)
- Values may have metadata (codeValue, displayName, definition)
- You want to query individuals via SPARQL using their type

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- Domain class for the enum values -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeCode">
    <skos:prefLabel>Sensor Type Code</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Typed individuals with metadata -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeAnalog">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/SensorTypeCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Analog</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Analog sensor type.</skos:definition>
    <example:codeValue>analog</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeDigital">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/SensorTypeCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Digital</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Digital sensor type.</skos:definition>
    <example:codeValue>digital</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction with owl:oneOf -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasSensorType"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom>
            <owl:Class>
                <owl:oneOf rdf:parseType="Collection">
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeAnalog"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/SensorTypeDigital"/>
                </owl:oneOf>
            </owl:Class>
        </owl:someValuesFrom>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
hasSensorType:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    type: string
    enum:
      - Analog
      - Digital
```

### Pattern A vs B Comparison

| Aspect | Pattern A | Pattern B |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Individual typing | `owl:NamedIndividual` only | Domain class + metadata |
| SPARQL query | Parse `owl:oneOf` | `?x a SensorTypeCode` |
| Metadata support | `rdfs:label` only | Full annotation support |
| API filtering | Not supported | Via `apiLabel` on individuals |

---

## Pattern C: Class-Based Taxonomy (Extensible)

Use Pattern C for extensible taxonomies where values can be added without modifying the restriction, and optionally support hierarchical structure.

### When to Use

- Values should be discoverable via class membership
- New values can be added without changing the schema
- Hierarchical grouping is needed (via subclasses)
- You want a reusable schema in components/schemas

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- Parent taxonomy class -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode">
    <skos:prefLabel>Environment Code</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Operating environment classification.</skos:definition>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Subclasses for hierarchy (optional) -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/IndoorEnvironment">
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Indoor Environment</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/OutdoorEnvironment">
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Outdoor Environment</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Individuals (instances of subclasses) -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/EnvOffice">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/IndoorEnvironment"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Office</skos:prefLabel>
    <example:codeValue>office</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/EnvOutdoor">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/OutdoorEnvironment"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Outdoor</skos:prefLabel>
    <example:codeValue>outdoor</example:codeValue>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction to named class (NOT owl:oneOf) -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasEnvironment"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/EnvironmentCode"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
# On the resource
hasEnvironment:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    $ref: '#/components/schemas/EnvironmentCode'

# In components/schemas
EnvironmentCode:
  type: object
  properties:
    code:
      type: string
      enum:
        - office
        - outdoor
    displayName:
      type: string
```

### Flat vs Hierarchical

Pattern C supports both flat taxonomies (direct instances of the class) and hierarchical taxonomies (instances of subclasses).

**Flat Taxonomy:**
```
EnvironmentCode
├── EnvOffice (instance)
├── EnvWarehouse (instance)
└── EnvOutdoor (instance)
```

**Hierarchical Taxonomy:**
```
EnvironmentCode
├── IndoorEnvironment (subclass)
│   ├── EnvOffice (instance)
│   └── EnvWarehouse (instance)
└── OutdoorEnvironment (subclass)
    └── EnvOutdoor (instance)
```

### Pattern B vs C Comparison

| Aspect | Pattern B | Pattern C |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Restriction target | Blank node with `owl:oneOf` | Named class IRI |
| Set closure | Closed (explicit list) | Open (query instances) |
| Adding values | Requires changing `owl:oneOf` | Just add instance |
| Hierarchy | Flat only | Supports subclass hierarchy |
| OpenAPI output | Inline enum | `$ref` to schema |

---

## Pattern D: SKOS Taxonomy

Use Pattern D for standards-compliant vocabularies using SKOS (Simple Knowledge Organization System), which enables interoperability with terminology servers and FHIR-compatible systems.

### When to Use

- Standards compliance required (SKOS, FHIR)
- Multi-language support needed (`skos:prefLabel` with `@lang`)
- Hierarchical relationships (`skos:broader`, `skos:narrower`)
- External code mapping (`skos:exactMatch`)
- Terminology server integration

### OWL Structure

```xml
<!-- SKOS ConceptScheme -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#ConceptScheme"/>
    <skos:prefLabel>Measurement Context Code System</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Bridge class linking to SKOS scheme -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode">
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
    <owl:equivalentClass>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#inScheme"/>
            <owl:hasValue rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </owl:equivalentClass>
    <skos:prefLabel>Measurement Context Code</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>

<!-- SKOS Concepts -->
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/ContextContinuous">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode"/>
    <skos:inScheme rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
    <skos:notation>continuous</skos:notation>
    <skos:prefLabel>Continuous</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Continuous measurement context.</skos:definition>
</owl:NamedIndividual>
<owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/ContextDiscrete">
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
    <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode"/>
    <skos:inScheme rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
    <skos:notation>discrete</skos:notation>
    <skos:prefLabel>Discrete</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>Discrete measurement context.</skos:definition>
    <skos:narrower rdf:resource="https://example.com/ContextTriggered"/>
</owl:NamedIndividual>

<!-- Restriction to SKOS-based class -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/hasMeasurementContext"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/MeasurementContextCode"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

### OpenAPI Output

```yaml
MeasurementContextCode:
  type: array
  minItems: 1
  items:
    type: string
    enum:
      - Continuous
      - Discrete
      - Triggered
```

### SKOS Detection Criteria

ASG detects Pattern D when the target class:
- Is `rdfs:subClassOf skos:Concept`, OR
- Has an `owl:equivalentClass` with a `skos:inScheme` restriction

### Pattern C vs D Comparison

| Aspect | Pattern C (Class-based) | Pattern D (SKOS) |
|--------|-------------------------|------------------|
| Standard | OWL only | OWL + SKOS |
| Hierarchy | `rdfs:subClassOf` | `skos:broader/narrower` |
| Membership | `rdf:type` | `skos:inScheme` |
| Multi-language | Not native | `skos:prefLabel@en` |
| External mapping | Not native | `skos:exactMatch` |
| Discovery | `?x a/rdfs:subClassOf* Class` | `?x skos:inScheme Scheme` |

---

## Cardinality Summary

All patterns support the same cardinality restrictions:

| OWL Restriction | Required? | Array? | minItems | maxItems |
|-----------------|-----------|--------|----------|----------|
| `qualifiedCardinality(1)` | Yes | No | - | - |
| `maxQualifiedCardinality(1)` | No | No | - | - |
| `someValuesFrom` | Yes | Yes | 1 | - |
| `allValuesFrom` | No | Yes | - | - |
| `minQualifiedCardinality(n)` | Yes (if n≥1) | Yes | n | - |
| `maxQualifiedCardinality(n)` | No | Yes | - | n |

---

## Choosing the Right Pattern

| Requirement | Recommended Pattern |
|-------------|---------------------|
| Simple closed list, rarely changes | Pattern A |
| Typed values with metadata | Pattern B |
| Extensible, add values without schema change | Pattern C |
| Hierarchical grouping needed | Pattern C or D |
| Standards compliance (SKOS, FHIR) | Pattern D |
| Multi-language support | Pattern D |
| External terminology mapping | Pattern D |

lines: +With `maxIndividualDepth >= 1` (default), Pattern B generates `oneOf` schemas with const objects:
+When Pattern B individuals have object property assertions, these are expanded as nested objects (up to `maxIndividualDepth`):
+If object property assertions form a cycle (A → B → A), ASG detects this and uses a string reference instead of infinite nesting.
+Set `maxIndividualDepth: 0` to generate simple string enums (pre-enhancement behavior):
| Individual typing | `owl:NamedIndividual` only | Domain class + metadata |
+| OpenAPI output | Simple string enum | `oneOf` with const objects |
+| Property assertions | Not supported | Expanded as nested objects |
-| Metadata support | `rdfs:label` only | Full annotation support |
-| API filtering | Not supported | Via `apiLabel` on individuals |
+| Metadata support | `rdfs:label` only | Full annotation + property support |

toolUseResult.newString:
<!-- Pattern B - Typed individuals with codeValue and property assertions -->
    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeAnalog">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeCode"/>
        <skos:notation>analog</skos:notation>
        <skos:prefLabel>Analog</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Analog sensor type.</skos:definition>
        <tp:codeValue>analog</tp:codeValue>
        <tp:voltage>5V</tp:voltage>
        <tp:hasCategory rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementCategory"/>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeDigital">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeCode"/>
        <skos:notation>digital</skos:notation>
        <skos:prefLabel>Digital</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Digital sensor type.</skos:definition>
        <tp:codeValue>digital</tp:codeValue>
        <tp:voltage>3.3V</tp:voltage>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeHybrid">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeCode"/>
        <skos:notation>hybrid</skos:notation>
        <skos:prefLabel>Hybrid</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Hybrid sensor type.</skos:definition>
        <tp:codeValue>hybrid</tp:codeValue>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <!-- Nested individual for object property expansion -->
    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementCategory">
        <skos:notation>measurement</skos:notation>
        <skos:prefLabel>Measurement</skos:prefLabel>
        <tp:unit>volts</tp:unit>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:AnnotationProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/codeValue"/>
    <owl:DatatypeProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/voltage"/>
    <owl:DatatypeProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/unit"/>
    <owl:ObjectProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasCategory"/>
toolUseResult.originalFile:
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<rdf:RDF xmlns="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/"
     xml:base="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/"
     xmlns:owl="http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#"
     xmlns:rdf="http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#"
     xmlns:xml="http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace"
     xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#"
     xmlns:rdfs="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#"
     xmlns:skos="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#"
     xmlns:base="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/"
     xmlns:api="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/"
     xmlns:tp="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/">

    <owl:Ontology rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/">
        <owl:imports rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/V1.0.2"/>
        <rdfs:comment>Test ontology for taxonomy pattern detection (Patterns A, B, C, D).</rdfs:comment>
    </owl:Ontology>

    <!-- APIRoot links to API specification -->
    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/APIRoot">
        <rdfs:subClassOf>
            <owl:Restriction>
                <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/hasAPI"/>
                <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0"/>
            </owl:Restriction>
        </rdfs:subClassOf>
    </rdf:Description>

    <!-- API Specification Class -->
    <owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0">
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/GRLAPISpecifications"/>
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:nodeID="hasPatternTestResource"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Taxonomy Patterns API V1.0</skos:prefLabel>
        <api:apiDisplayLabel>TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0</api:apiDisplayLabel>
    </owl:Class>

    <owl:Restriction rdf:nodeID="hasPatternTestResource">
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/hasResourceSchema"/>
        <owl:qualifiedCardinality rdf:datatype="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#nonNegativeInteger">1</owl:qualifiedCardinality>
        <owl:onClass rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/PatternTestResource"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
    <owl:Axiom>
        <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0"/>
        <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
        <owl:annotatedTarget rdf:nodeID="hasPatternTestResource"/>
        <api:apitag>PatternTestResource</api:apitag>
    </owl:Axiom>

    <!-- ================================================================== -->
    <!-- PATTERN A: Inline enumeration with untyped individuals             -->
    <!-- ================================================================== -->

    <!-- Pattern A - Untyped individuals (only owl:NamedIndividual) -->
    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/Active">
        <rdfs:label>Active</rdfs:label>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/Inactive">
        <rdfs:label>Inactive</rdfs:label>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/Pending">
        <rdfs:label>Pending</rdfs:label>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:ObjectProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasStatusA">
        <skos:prefLabel>Has Status A</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Pattern A: Status using untyped individuals.</skos:definition>
    </owl:ObjectProperty>

    <!-- ================================================================== -->
    <!-- PATTERN B: Typed individuals with metadata                         -->
    <!-- ================================================================== -->

    <!-- Pattern B - Domain class for typed individuals -->
    <owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeCode">
        <skos:prefLabel>Sensor Type Code</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Code values for sensor types.</skos:definition>
    </owl:Class>

    <!-- Pattern B - Typed individuals with codeValue -->
    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeAnalog">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeCode"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Analog</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Analog sensor type.</skos:definition>
        <tp:codeValue>analog</tp:codeValue>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeDigital">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeCode"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Digital</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Digital sensor type.</skos:definition>
        <tp:codeValue>digital</tp:codeValue>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeHybrid">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeCode"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Hybrid</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Hybrid sensor type.</skos:definition>
        <tp:codeValue>hybrid</tp:codeValue>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:AnnotationProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/codeValue"/>

    <owl:ObjectProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasSensorType">
        <skos:prefLabel>Has Sensor Type</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Pattern B: Sensor type using typed individuals.</skos:definition>
    </owl:ObjectProperty>

    <!-- ================================================================== -->
    <!-- PATTERN C: Class-based taxonomy (extensible)                       -->
    <!-- ================================================================== -->

    <!-- Pattern C - Taxonomy class -->
    <owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/EnvironmentCode">
        <skos:prefLabel>Environment Code</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Operating environment classification.</skos:definition>
    </owl:Class>

    <!-- Pattern C - Subclasses for hierarchy -->
    <owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/IndoorEnvironment">
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/EnvironmentCode"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Indoor Environment</skos:prefLabel>
    </owl:Class>

    <owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/OutdoorEnvironment">
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/EnvironmentCode"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Outdoor Environment</skos:prefLabel>
    </owl:Class>

    <!-- Pattern C - Individuals -->
    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/EnvOffice">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/IndoorEnvironment"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Office</skos:prefLabel>
        <tp:codeValue>office</tp:codeValue>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/EnvWarehouse">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/IndoorEnvironment"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Warehouse</skos:prefLabel>
        <tp:codeValue>warehouse</tp:codeValue>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/EnvOutdoor">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/OutdoorEnvironment"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Outdoor</skos:prefLabel>
        <tp:codeValue>outdoor</tp:codeValue>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:ObjectProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasEnvironment">
        <skos:prefLabel>Has Environment</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Pattern C: Environment using class-based taxonomy.</skos:definition>
    </owl:ObjectProperty>

    <!-- ================================================================== -->
    <!-- PATTERN D: SKOS taxonomy                                           -->
    <!-- ================================================================== -->

    <!-- Pattern D - SKOS ConceptScheme -->
    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextScheme">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#ConceptScheme"/>
        <skos:prefLabel>Measurement Context Code System</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Code system for measurement context types.</skos:definition>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <!-- Pattern D - Bridge class linking to SKOS scheme -->
    <owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextCode">
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
        <owl:equivalentClass>
            <owl:Restriction>
                <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#inScheme"/>
                <owl:hasValue rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
            </owl:Restriction>
        </owl:equivalentClass>
        <skos:prefLabel>Measurement Context Code</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>SKOS-based measurement context codes.</skos:definition>
    </owl:Class>

    <!-- Pattern D - SKOS Concepts -->
    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/ContextContinuous">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextCode"/>
        <skos:inScheme rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
        <skos:notation>continuous</skos:notation>
        <skos:prefLabel>Continuous</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Continuous measurement context.</skos:definition>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/ContextDiscrete">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextCode"/>
        <skos:inScheme rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
        <skos:notation>discrete</skos:notation>
        <skos:prefLabel>Discrete</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Discrete measurement context.</skos:definition>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:NamedIndividual rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/ContextTriggered">
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#Concept"/>
        <rdf:type rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextCode"/>
        <skos:inScheme rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextScheme"/>
        <skos:notation>triggered</skos:notation>
        <skos:prefLabel>Triggered</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Event-triggered measurement context.</skos:definition>
        <skos:broader rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/ContextDiscrete"/>
    </owl:NamedIndividual>

    <owl:ObjectProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasMeasurementContext">
        <skos:prefLabel>Has Measurement Context</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Pattern D: Measurement context using SKOS taxonomy.</skos:definition>
    </owl:ObjectProperty>

    <!-- ================================================================== -->
    <!-- MAIN RESOURCE CLASS                                                -->
    <!-- ================================================================== -->

    <owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/PatternTestResource">
        <skos:prefLabel>Pattern Test Resource</skos:prefLabel>
        <skos:definition>Resource class to test all taxonomy patterns.</skos:definition>

        <!-- apiID -->
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:nodeID="patternApiId"/>

        <!-- Pattern A: owl:oneOf with untyped individuals -->
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:nodeID="hasStatusARestr"/>

        <!-- Pattern B: owl:oneOf with typed individuals -->
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:nodeID="hasSensorTypeRestr"/>

        <!-- Pattern C: someValuesFrom named class (extensible) -->
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:nodeID="hasEnvironmentRestr"/>

        <!-- Pattern D: someValuesFrom SKOS-based class -->
        <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:nodeID="hasMeasurementContextRestr"/>
    </owl:Class>

    <!-- apiID restriction -->
    <owl:Restriction rdf:nodeID="patternApiId">
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/apiID"/>
        <owl:qualifiedCardinality rdf:datatype="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#nonNegativeInteger">1</owl:qualifiedCardinality>
        <owl:onDataRange rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
    <owl:Axiom>
        <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/PatternTestResource"/>
        <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
        <owl:annotatedTarget rdf:nodeID="patternApiId"/>
        <api:apiLabel>TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0</api:apiLabel>
    </owl:Axiom>

    <!-- Pattern A: hasStatusA restriction with owl:oneOf (untyped individuals) -->
    <owl:Restriction rdf:nodeID="hasStatusARestr">
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasStatusA"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom>
            <owl:Class>
                <owl:oneOf rdf:parseType="Collection">
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/Active"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/Inactive"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/Pending"/>
                </owl:oneOf>
            </owl:Class>
        </owl:someValuesFrom>
    </owl:Restriction>
    <owl:Axiom>
        <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/PatternTestResource"/>
        <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
        <owl:annotatedTarget rdf:nodeID="hasStatusARestr"/>
        <api:apiLabel>TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0</api:apiLabel>
    </owl:Axiom>

    <!-- Pattern B: hasSensorType restriction with owl:oneOf (typed individuals) -->
    <owl:Restriction rdf:nodeID="hasSensorTypeRestr">
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasSensorType"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom>
            <owl:Class>
                <owl:oneOf rdf:parseType="Collection">
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeAnalog"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeDigital"/>
                    <rdf:Description rdf:about="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/SensorTypeHybrid"/>
                </owl:oneOf>
            </owl:Class>
        </owl:someValuesFrom>
    </owl:Restriction>
    <owl:Axiom>
        <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/PatternTestResource"/>
        <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
        <owl:annotatedTarget rdf:nodeID="hasSensorTypeRestr"/>
        <api:apiLabel>TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0</api:apiLabel>
    </owl:Axiom>

    <!-- Pattern C: hasEnvironment restriction (someValuesFrom named class) -->
    <owl:Restriction rdf:nodeID="hasEnvironmentRestr">
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasEnvironment"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/EnvironmentCode"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
    <owl:Axiom>
        <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/PatternTestResource"/>
        <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
        <owl:annotatedTarget rdf:nodeID="hasEnvironmentRestr"/>
        <api:apiLabel>TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0</api:apiLabel>
    </owl:Axiom>

    <!-- Pattern D: hasMeasurementContext restriction (someValuesFrom SKOS class) -->
    <owl:Restriction rdf:nodeID="hasMeasurementContextRestr">
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/hasMeasurementContext"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/MeasurementContextCode"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
    <owl:Axiom>
        <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/PatternTestResource"/>
        <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
        <owl:annotatedTarget rdf:nodeID="hasMeasurementContextRestr"/>
        <api:apiLabel>TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0</api:apiLabel>
    </owl:Axiom>

</rdf:RDF>

lines: -      // Pattern B: hasSensorType should create enum from typed individuals
+      // Pattern B: hasSensorType should create oneOf from typed individuals
+      // Pattern B with maxIndividualDepth >= 1 generates oneOf with const objects
+      // Each individual in oneOf should have a code property
+      assertNotNull(code, "First individual should have code const");
+          "Code should be one of the expected values");
+      // Individuals with data property assertions should have those properties
+      // At least one individual should have voltage property (Analog and Digital have it)
+          // Property doesn't exist for this individual
+      assertTrue(hasVoltage, "At least one individual should have voltage property");
+      // SensorTypeAnalog has hasCategory pointing to MeasurementCategory
+      // This should be expanded as a nested object
+      // Find the analog individual and check for nested hasCategory
+            // Check if hasCategory is an object with properties
+              // Could also be a nested object without explicit type
+      assertTrue(hasNestedCategory, "Analog individual should have nested hasCategory object");

Based on the search results, here's information about using `EntitySearcher.getDataPropertyValues()` with the OWL API and Multimap:

## EntitySearcher.getDataPropertyValues() in OWL API

The `EntitySearcher` class provides static methods for retrieving data property values from OWL ontologies. The method `getDataPropertyValues(OWLIndividual i, OWLOntology ontology)` returns a `com.google.common.collect.Multimap<OWLDataPropertyExpression,OWLLiteral>` and takes parameters: `i` - individual and `ontology` - ontology to search.

### Method Signatures

**OWL API 5.x:**
There are also overloaded versions that return `Stream<OWLLiteral>` when you specify a specific data property expression: `getDataPropertyValues(OWLIndividual i, OWLDataPropertyExpression p, OWLOntology ontology)`.

**OWL API 4.x:**
The version 4 API provides `Collection<OWLLiteral> getDataPropertyValues(@Nonnull OWLIndividual i, @Nonnull OWLDataPropertyExpression p, @Nonnull OWLOntology ontology)` which returns a Collection instead of a Stream.

### Migration from OWL API 3.x to 4.x

When migrating from OWL API 3.x to 4.x, the old pattern `Map<OWLDataPropertyExpression, Set<OWLLiteral>> result = ind.getDataPropertyValues(ont);` should be changed to `Multimap<OWLDataPropertyExpression, OWLLiteral> result = EntitySearcher.getDataPropertyValues(ind, ont);`.

### Example Usage with Reasoner

For getting data property values using a reasoner, you can use code like:
```java
OWLDataProperty name = manager.getOWLDataFactory().getOWLDataProperty(IRI.create("URI_of your DataProperty"));
OWLNamedIndividual ind = sameInd.getRepresentativeElement();
Set<OWLLiteral> names = reasoner.getDataPropertyValues(ind, name);
```

### Import Statements

The OWL API examples show the relevant imports: `import static org.semanticweb.owlapi.search.EntitySearcher.getAnnotationObjects;` and similar static imports from `org.semanticweb.owlapi.search.Searcher`.

### Alternative: Using Searcher Class

The `Searcher` class also provides `values(Stream<OWLDataPropertyAssertionAxiom> axioms, OWLDataPropertyExpression p)` which retrieves literals from a collection of assertions.

results:
Based on the search results, here's information about using `EntitySearcher.getDataPropertyValues()` with the OWL API and Multimap:

## EntitySearcher.getDataPropertyValues() in OWL API

The `EntitySearcher` class provides static methods for retrieving data property values from OWL ontologies. The method `getDataPropertyValues(OWLIndividual i, OWLOntology ontology)` returns a `com.google.common.collect.Multimap<OWLDataPropertyExpression,OWLLiteral>` and takes parameters: `i` - individual and `ontology` - ontology to search.

### Method Signatures

**OWL API 5.x:**
There are also overloaded versions that return `Stream<OWLLiteral>` when you specify a specific data property expression: `getDataPropertyValues(OWLIndividual i, OWLDataPropertyExpression p, OWLOntology ontology)`.

**OWL API 4.x:**
The version 4 API provides `Collection<OWLLiteral> getDataPropertyValues(@Nonnull OWLIndividual i, @Nonnull OWLDataPropertyExpression p, @Nonnull OWLOntology ontology)` which returns a Collection instead of a Stream.

### Migration from OWL API 3.x to 4.x

When migrating from OWL API 3.x to 4.x, the old pattern `Map<OWLDataPropertyExpression, Set<OWLLiteral>> result = ind.getDataPropertyValues(ont);` should be changed to `Multimap<OWLDataPropertyExpression, OWLLiteral> result = EntitySearcher.getDataPropertyValues(ind, ont);`.

### Example Usage with Reasoner

For getting data property values using a reasoner, you can use code like:
```java
OWLDataProperty name = manager.getOWLDataFactory().getOWLDataProperty(IRI.create("URI_of your DataProperty"));
OWLNamedIndividual ind = sameInd.getRepresentativeElement();
Set<OWLLiteral> names = reasoner.getDataPropertyValues(ind, name);
```

### Import Statements

The OWL API examples show the relevant imports: `import static org.semanticweb.owlapi.search.EntitySearcher.getAnnotationObjects;` and similar static imports from `org.semanticweb.owlapi.search.Searcher`.

### Alternative: Using Searcher Class

The `Searcher` class also provides `values(Stream<OWLDataPropertyAssertionAxiom> axioms, OWLDataPropertyExpression p)` which retrieves literals from a collection of assertions.

content.text:
Good, maxIndividualDepth is 2. The issue is that the SchemaVisitor is extracting individualGraphs but they aren't being passed properly through the system. Let me check the mapping flow - the issue is likely that TaxonomyPropertyMapper is using the old interface that doesn't preserve the graphs.

Let me look at how TaxonomyPropertyMapper gets the TaxonomyInfo:

OK, so the visitors visit the super class expressions and during that visitation, `detectTaxonomyPattern` should be called. The issue is that `detectTaxonomyPattern` is called when we visit things like `OWLObjectSomeValuesFrom`, but it requires the individuals to already have property assertions in the ontology.

content.text:
The ontology has `tp:voltage>5V</tp:voltage>` which is just a plain XML property - it won't be recognized as a data property assertion by the OWL API unless we declare it properly. The issue is that OWL/RDF needs the assertion to be declared with the right syntax.

Looking at the OWL parsing, when I use `ontology.dataPropertyAssertionAxioms(individual)`, it returns the axioms that were explicitly parsed as DataPropertyAssertion in OWL. But the RDF/XML format uses `<tp:voltage>5V</tp:voltage>` which gets interpreted as an annotation or a generic triple, not necessarily a DataPropertyAssertionAxiom.

content.text:
I see the property declarations have the full namespace `https://example.com/taxonomy-patterns/voltage` but the assertions in the individual use just `<voltage>5V</voltage>` without a namespace - these may be parsed as annotation properties rather than data property assertions. 

The OWL API interprets RDF differently based on whether properties are declared. When you write `<voltage>5V</voltage>` without the namespace prefix, and voltage is declared as an owl:DatatypeProperty, the OWL API might not match them correctly.

Let me check if there are any DataPropertyAssertion axioms in the merged ontology:
No explicit DataPropertyAssertion. Let me try running with verbose output to see what's happening:

content.text:
The flat ontology **does** have the DataPropertyAssertion and ObjectPropertyAssertion! So the issue is that the code is working on the wrong ontology - it's using the original (pre-flattened) ontology.

Let me trace through where the SchemaVisitor gets its ontology. The issue is that `extractIndividualGraph` is called during the visitor traversal, which happens on the original ontology before flattening.

Looking at the pipeline:
1. Original ontology loaded 
2. SchemaVisitor runs on the classes (extracting taxonomy patterns)
3. Ontology is flattened
4. TaxonomyPatternMapper generates schemas

The SchemaVisitor is using the pre-flattened ontology. The assertions are there in the flattened ontology, but not recognized in the original because the namespaces are different.

This is a fundamental issue with when the visitor runs. The visitor needs to run on the flattened ontology, not the original.

Let me check if the context.getOntology() returns the flattened one:

content.text:
I see line 240-241:
```java
.filter(axiom -> !axioms.taxonomy().contains(axiom))
```

This **excludes** taxonomy axioms from the visitor! The taxonomy axioms are handled separately via `processTaxonomyAxioms()`. But the taxonomy detection in SchemaVisitor's `detectTaxonomyPattern()` method happens during the visitor traversal.

So taxonomyAxioms (which include hasSensorType) are explicitly filtered OUT of the visitor traversal, meaning `detectTaxonomyPattern` is never called for them.

Let me look at how taxonomyAxioms are identified:

content.content: No matches found

content.text: I'll continue working on fixing the architecture issue where taxonomy axioms bypass the SchemaVisitor's detectTaxonomyPattern. Let me first review the current state of the code to understand the flow better.

Now I understand the architecture better. The issue is clear: on line 240-241, taxonomy axioms are filtered out from the visitor processing:

```java
axioms.subClass().stream()
    .filter(axiom -> !axioms.taxonomy().contains(axiom))  // <-- excludes taxonomy axioms!
```

This means taxonomy axioms never reach the SchemaVisitor's `accept()` method where `detectTaxonomyPattern` is called. The solution is to also pass taxonomy axioms through the visitor so they can be analyzed for patterns.

Let me check how `getTaxonomyAxioms` determines what counts as a taxonomy axiom:

Now I understand the full picture. The `detectTaxonomyPattern` method is called from:
1. `visit(OWLObjectSomeValuesFrom ce)` - line 166
2. `visit(OWLObjectAllValuesFrom ce)` - line 193  
3. One other location (line 377)

But these methods are only invoked when the axiom's superclass expression is `accept()`ed by the visitor. Since taxonomy axioms are filtered out in `visitAxiomsWithVisitors` (line 241), they never get to call `detectTaxonomyPattern`.

The fix should be to also visit taxonomy axioms through the visitor so that `detectTaxonomyPattern` gets called. Let me modify `ResourceSchemaMapper.visitAxiomsWithVisitors` to also include taxonomy axioms:

Progress! Now `detectTaxonomyPattern` IS being called for `hasSensorType` and Pattern B is being detected with 3 graphs. The issues now are:

1. Pattern B tests: The code value isn't matching expected values (likely the code is using IRI local name instead of skos:notation)
2. Pattern D tests: MeasurementContextCode property is missing entirely

Let me check the generated output to understand what's happening:

openapi: 3.1.0
info:
  title: TaxonomyPatternsAPIV1.0
  description: "Taxonomy Patterns Test API - Tests Patterns A, B, C, D"
  version: 1.0.0

  description: Resource class to test all taxonomy patterns.
paths:

    get:
      tags:
      - PatternTestResource
      description: Fetch an item given a known id
      operationId: PatternTestResource/single/GET
      parameters:

          type: string
      responses:
        "401":

        "403":

        "404":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/404"
        "406":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/406"
        "429":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/429"
        "500":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/500"
        "200":
          description: Existing PatternTestResource
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"
              example:
                apiID: apiID string value
                hasSensorType:
                - code: string
                  hasCategory:
                    code: string
                    displayName: string
                hasStatusA:
                - Active
                hasMeasurementContext:
                - {}
                hasEnvironment:
                - {}
    put:
      tags:
      - PatternTestResource
      description: "Update an item given a known id. NOTE: the unique identifier will\
        \ not be updated by this operation"
      operationId: PatternTestResource/single/PUT
      parameters:
      - name: PatternTestResourceId
        in: path
        required: true
        schema:
          type: string
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"
            example:
              apiID: apiID string value
              hasSensorType:
              - code: string
                hasCategory:
                  code: string
                  displayName: string
              hasStatusA:
              - Active
              hasMeasurementContext:
              - {}
              hasEnvironment:
              - {}
      responses:
        "400":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/400"
        "401":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/401"
        "403":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/403"
        "404":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/404"
        "406":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/406"
        "429":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/429"
        "500":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/500"
        "200":
          description: Existing PatternTestResource
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"
              example:
                apiID: apiID string value
                hasSensorType:
                - code: string
                  hasCategory:
                    code: string
                    displayName: string
                hasStatusA:
                - Active
                hasMeasurementContext:
                - {}
                hasEnvironment:
                - {}
    delete:
      tags:
      - PatternTestResource
      description: Remove an item given a known id
      operationId: PatternTestResource/single/DELETE
      parameters:
      - name: PatternTestResourceId
        in: path
        required: true
        schema:
          type: string
      responses:
        "401":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/401"
        "403":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/403"

        "200":

                - {}

    patch:

      parameters:

        in: path

          type: string

        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"
            example:
              apiID: apiID string value

        "200":

                $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"

                apiID: apiID string value
                hasSensorType:
                - code: string
                  hasCategory:
                    code: string
                    displayName: string
                hasStatusA:
                - Active
                hasMeasurementContext:
                - {}
                hasEnvironment:
                - {}
  /PatternTestResource:
    summary: Collection of Pattern Test Resource
    description: Resource class to test all taxonomy patterns.
    get:
      tags:
      - PatternTestResource
      description: Fetch a list of items.
      operationId: PatternTestResource/collection/GET
      parameters:
      - name: start
        in: query
        description: Index of start item
        schema:
          type: integer
          format: int32
      - name: limit
        in: query
        description: Maximum items to return
        schema:
          type: integer
          format: int32

        "429":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/429"
        "500":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/500"
        "200":
          description: PatternTestResource Collection
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  totalItems:
                    type: integer
                    format: int32

                    example: 3
                  items:
                    type: array
                    items:
                      $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"
              example:
                totalItems: 3
                items:
                - apiID: apiID string value
                  hasSensorType:
                  - code: string
                    hasCategory:
                      code: string
                      displayName: string
                  hasStatusA:
                  - Active
                  hasMeasurementContext:
                  - {}
                  hasEnvironment:
                  - {}
    post:
      tags:
      - PatternTestResource
      description: "Create a new item. NOTE: Any unique identifier in the request\
        \ body will be ignored"
      operationId: PatternTestResource/collection/POST
      requestBody:
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"
            example:
              apiID: apiID string value
              hasSensorType:
              - code: string
                hasCategory:
                  code: string
                  displayName: string
              hasStatusA:
              - Active
              hasMeasurementContext:
              - {}
              hasEnvironment:
              - {}
      responses:
        "400":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/400"
        "401":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/401"
        "403":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/403"
        "406":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/406"
        "429":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/429"
        "500":
          $ref: "#/components/responses/500"
        "201":
          description: New PatternTestResource
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/PatternTestResource"
              example:
                apiID: apiID string value
                hasSensorType:
                - code: string
                  hasCategory:
                    code: string
                    displayName: string
                hasStatusA:
                - Active
                hasMeasurementContext:
                - {}
                hasEnvironment:
                - {}
components:
  schemas:
    PatternTestResource:
      type: object
      additionalProperties: false
      description: Resource class to test all taxonomy patterns.
      properties:
        apiID:
          type: string
          description: REST ID of the object.
          example: apiID string value
          title: Api Id
        hasSensorType:
          type: array
          items:
            description: "Pattern B: Sensor type using typed individuals."
            oneOf:
            - type: object
              properties:
                code:
                  type: string
                  const: SensorTypeAnalog
                voltage:
                  const: 5V
                hasCategory:
                  type: object
                  properties:
                    code:
                      type: string
                      const: measurement
                    displayName:
                      type: string
                      const: Measurement
                    unit:
                      const: volts
            - type: object
              properties:
                code:
                  type: string
                  const: SensorTypeDigital
                voltage:
                  const: 3.3V
            - type: object
              properties:
                code:
                  type: string
                  const: SensorTypeHybrid
          minItems: 1
          title: Has Sensor Type
        hasStatusA:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
            description: "Pattern A: Status using untyped individuals."
            enum:
            - Active
            - Inactive
            - Pending
          minItems: 1
          title: Has Status A
        hasMeasurementContext:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/MeasurementContextCode"
          minItems: 1
          title: Has Measurement Context
        hasEnvironment:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/EnvironmentCode"
          minItems: 1
          title: Has Environment
      required:
      - apiID
      - hasEnvironment
      - hasEnvironment
      - hasMeasurementContext
      - hasMeasurementContext
      - hasSensorType
      - hasSensorType
      - hasStatusA
      - hasStatusA
      title: Pattern Test Resource
    EnvironmentCode:
      type: object
      additionalProperties: false
      description: Operating environment classification.
      title: Environment Code
    MeasurementContextCode:
      type: object
      additionalProperties: false
      description: SKOS-based measurement context codes.
      title: Measurement Context Code
  responses:
    "400":
      description: Bad Request
    "401":
      description: Unauthorized
    "403":
      description: Forbidden
    "404":
      description: Not found.
    "405":
      description: Method Not Allowed
    "406":
      description: Not acceptable
    "429":
      description: Too Many Requests
    "500":
      description: Internal Server Error

content.text:
Excellent! The output is now much better. I can see:

1. **hasSensorType** (Pattern B) now generates a `oneOf` with const objects - lines 362-397
2. It has nested objects for `hasCategory` with `code`, `displayName`, and `unit` - lines 374-384
3. The data property `voltage` is included - lines 372-373, 390-391

The issues are:

1. **Pattern B code values** - Using IRI local names like "SensorTypeAnalog" instead of the expected skos:notation values. I need to check if the ontology has skos:notation annotations.
2. **Pattern D (MeasurementContextCode)** - The property is named `hasMeasurementContext` not `MeasurementContextCode`. The tests are looking for the wrong property name.

Let me check the test file to understand what the tests expect:

I see the issues now:

1. **Pattern B test** (`patternBIndividualsHaveCodeProperty` at line 108) expects code values like "analog", "digital", "hybrid" but we're generating "SensorTypeAnalog", "SensorTypeDigital", "SensorTypeHybrid" (IRI local names instead of skos:notation)

2. **Pattern D tests** expect the property name to be `MeasurementContextCode` but the actual generated property is `hasMeasurementContext` (which is the property name, not the class name)

Let me check the ontology to understand the expected patterns:
